title: Spacetime positive mass theorem
speaker: Lan-Hsuan Huang
affiliation: University of Connecticut; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: Abstract: The spacetime positive mass theorem says that an asymptotically flat initial data set with the dominant energy condition must have a timelike energy-momentum vector, unless the initial data set is in the Minkowski spacetime. We will review backgrounds and recent progress toward this statement.
tags: Workshop on Geometric Functionals: Analysis and Applications

proof_stages.label: Equality-case rigidity theorem and geometric meaning
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the theorem for complete asymptotically flat initial data sets in dimensions 3 through 7 satisfying the dominant energy condition and equality in the ADM energy-momentum inequality, and explains that the conclusion is vanishing ADM energy and momentum, equivalently flat Minkowski spacetime rigidity rather than uniqueness of the hypersurface data themselves.

proof_stages.label: Positive mass theorem input and dimension restriction
proof_stages.summary: The proof relies on the already-established spacetime positive mass theorem in dimensions below 8, which gives E >= |P| and later supplies the local-minimizing inequality needed in the constrained variational argument.

proof_stages.label: Riemannian scalar-curvature deformation model
proof_stages.summary: The time-symmetric case k=0 is reviewed as a scalar-curvature rigidity problem, and the Fisher-Marsden/Covino deformation theory is used to show how failure to locally increase scalar curvature forces a nontrivial kernel of the formal adjoint of the scalar-curvature linearization.

proof_stages.label: Dominant-energy constraint map and modified deformation theory
proof_stages.summary: The Einstein constraint map Phi(g,k)=(2mu,J) is introduced so that the dominant energy condition is mu >= |J|, and a modified constraint map is designed to overcome the nondifferentiability and moving-norm difficulties in perturbing the dominant energy condition.

proof_stages.label: Adjoint-kernel approach and stationary-vacuum dead end
proof_stages.summary: Following the scalar-curvature strategy suggests that equality should imply a nontrivial kernel of the modified adjoint constraint operator, but interpreting such kernels would require proving vacuum/stationary structure and engaging difficult stationary uniqueness and Bartnik-type conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Prescribed-asymptotics variational functional
proof_stages.summary: To avoid full stationary classification, the speaker adopts a Bartnik-inspired plan to solve the adjoint equation with prescribed asymptotics by fixing a lapse-shift pair and defining a functional combining ADM energy, ADM momentum, and the modified constraint map so that boundary terms cancel in the first variation.

proof_stages.label: Constrained minimization and Lagrange multiplier
proof_stages.summary: Under the modified constraint, the dominant energy condition and Cauchy-Schwarz make the original data a local minimizer by positivity of mass, and a decaying Lagrange multiplier then corrects the prescribed lapse-shift pair to a true adjoint solution that feeds into the flatness and ADM-zero conclusion.

examples.name: Time-symmetric Riemannian case k=0
examples.note: The special case k=0 turns the dominant energy condition into nonnegative scalar curvature and serves as the model from which the deformation-and-kernel strategy for the spacetime case is drawn.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes to k=0, derives the DEC as nonnegative scalar curvature with p=0, and later decouples the adjoint equations in this case

passes.reason: Setting k=0 collapses the general initial-data constraints to the Riemannian scalar-curvature case.
the case k=0 removes the extrinsic-curvature part of the initial data and is used as a reduction, not as a refutation
The case k=0 is the time-symmetric/trivial second-fundamental-form subcase of initial data, used as a reduction rather than to refute a claim.
The condition k=0 is the time-symmetric/trivial-extrinsic-curvature subcase used as a standard reduction, not as a refutation.
The case k=0 is a time-symmetric reduction with the extrinsic-curvature tensor collapsed to zero, used as a special positive case rather than a refutation.
k is identically zero, collapsing the general spacetime initial-data problem to the time-symmetric Riemannian scalar-curvature case.
The case k=0 collapses the spacetime initial data to the time-symmetric Riemannian scalar-curvature setting and is used as a special case, not a disproof.
The condition k=0 removes the extrinsic-curvature data and serves as the time-symmetric Riemannian special case, not as a refutation.
The case k=0 is the time-symmetric reduction with the extrinsic-curvature tensor collapsed to zero, used as a special case rather than to refute a claim.
The condition k=0 is the zero-extrinsic-curvature Riemannian reduction used as a benchmark, not as a refutation.
The case k=0 is the zero-extrinsic-curvature/time-symmetric reduction to ordinary scalar curvature, used as a special case rather than a refutation.
It is used as a positive special case, with the extrinsic-curvature tensor k set identically to zero.

examples.name: Euclidean metric with a positive-scalar-curvature bump in a ball
examples.note: The thought experiment of trying to make scalar curvature positive inside a ball while leaving Euclidean space unchanged at infinity makes mass-zero rigidity visible as a local deformation obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the Riemannian rigidity theorem to the proposed Euclidean metric with a compact scalar-curvature bump and concludes it cannot exist because the mass at infinity stays zero

passes.reason: Such a compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump would keep ADM mass zero, the positive-mass equality value, and is posed as the forbidden adversarial construction.
a compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump would leave the ADM mass at the zero/equality boundary and is invoked as an impossible obstruction
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump would keep ADM mass zero, the positive-mass equality extreme, and is used as the forbidden obstruction to local bumping.
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump would keep the Euclidean ADM mass at the zero/equality boundary and is presented as the adversarial construction rigidity forbids.
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump while remaining Euclidean at infinity would be a nonflat zero-ADM-mass equality case, invoked as the forbidden deformation ruled out by rigidity.
a compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump would keep the ADM mass at the zero-mass boundary and is presented as the forbidden adversarial case ruled out by rigidity.
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump preserving Euclidean infinity is presented as the forbidden enemy construction at the zero-mass boundary.
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump on Euclidean space is presented as the forbidden enemy construction, and exact Euclidean behavior at infinity would put it at zero ADM mass.
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump while staying Euclidean at infinity is the forbidden enemy construction, and it would have zero ADM mass at the rigidity boundary.
A compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump with Euclidean exterior would have zero ADM mass, the positive-mass equality boundary, and is presented as the forbidden enemy construction.
The putative compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature bump would keep ADM mass at the zero boundary and is introduced as the forbidden deformation.
It is the forbidden compactly supported positive-scalar-curvature deformation that would keep the ADM mass at the zero lower bound.

examples.name: Spherical and hemisphere non-rigidity phenomena
examples.note: The hemisphere examples are cited as a contrast showing that spherical scalar-curvature rigidity is more delicate than the Euclidean or hyperbolic analogues.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only notes spherical delicacy and hemisphere non-rigidity examples by others without analyzing them

examples.name: Euclidean-space kernel of the scalar-curvature adjoint
examples.note: On flat Euclidean space the adjoint equation forces the test function to have zero Hessian, hence to be constant or linear, illustrating the finite-dimensional nature of the obstruction space.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the Euclidean adjoint-kernel equation, traces it to get harmonicity, and concludes the Hessian vanishes so the functions are constant or linear

passes.reason: On Euclidean space the scalar-curvature adjoint kernel consists of affine functions and attains the general n+1 dimensional upper bound.
on Euclidean space the adjoint kernel is the affine functions and attains the general n+1-dimensional upper bound
On maximally symmetric Euclidean space the scalar-curvature-adjoint kernel is the affine functions, attaining the maximal n+1-dimensional kernel.

examples.name: Euclidean initial data and Killing fields
examples.note: For the full constraint adjoint on flat initial data, the speaker identifies the lapse part with constants and linear functions and the shift part with Euclidean translations and rotations, illustrating the symmetry interpretation of kernel elements.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker verifies the Euclidean initial-data kernel by identifying f as constant/linear and x as Euclidean Killing fields, namely translations and rotations

passes.reason: Flat Euclidean initial data has k=0 and realizes the maximal Killing-field/kernel symmetry of the model Minkowski data.
Euclidean initial data have k=0 and maximal flat symmetry, and their adjoint kernel reaches the finite-dimensional upper bound via translations and rotations
Flat Euclidean data with k=0 is the trivial time-symmetric model and has the full Euclidean Killing algebra, attaining the maximal KID dimension.
The flat k=0 Euclidean initial data are time-symmetric with trivial extrinsic curvature and have maximal Euclidean Killing/constraint-kernel symmetry.
The flat Euclidean initial data have k=0 and the maximal Euclidean Killing algebra, attaining the general kernel-dimension bound in this illustrative model case.
flat Euclidean initial data have k=0 and their adjoint kernel is the maximal Killing-field space of translations and rotations.
